Um, so our,
our focus at the commission is on the long-term interest
of consumers, and we think that having ERs like this
is consistent with that aim.
Um, I think in any,
or in in lots of economic regulatory regimes
around the world where you have a five year period within
which we set the maximum revenue that an entity can earn
and the minimum level of reliability that they have
to deliver, the, the, there has to be some sort
of change mechanisms
because certain, certain, uh,
circumstances change over that period.
And you can have severe weather events such as the cyclone,
um, that affected the East Coast.
So having ERs is a fairly normal thing
in network regulation.
And we think that having them is in the consumer interest
because, uh, say in the case for catastrophic event,
if we didn't have ERs,
and probably one of two things would happen, either
the regulated entity would price in the risk to the prices
that they charge retailers and then, and consumers
and consumers might end up paying for that risk,
even if the risk doesn't eventuate
or the regulated entity might do nothing.
And then you would have a circumstance, say with the cyclone
where reliability on the network suffers
and they wait until the next regulatory period,
the next five year period to try
to get the revenue to invest in the network.
So we think having this kind of mechanism allows us
to scrutinize the expenditure in consumer's interests
and make sure consumers get value for money.
We recognize that that unfortunately has a bill impact
for people who may already be, be struggling with the size
of their electricity bills, but we think it's more efficient
and allows us to scrutinize the expenditure in a way which
is better than the alternatives.
So we think ERs are in consumer's interest.
In the case of this reopener,
we did scrutinize the expenditure, uh,
first like commissioned an external expert

and we consulted before making a final decision.

electricity distribution businesses on these ERs,

a default price quality path
